author: @adus123
text: I think the cost and space needed is still a big issue. Putting them thrush on your roof is a lot more simple. Unless you have a field to put them in which case you could have extra panels. Or have some west and east.

author: @benjaminhildebrand2220
text: I think a windup clock mechanism that I could set in the morning would work quite well...

author: @1quasar1
text: I've had this idea for some time now.  It involves using a super capacitor (let's say 60 vdc @ 60 Farads) in between panels and an MPPT controller, in order to eliminate cloud shading interruptions etc. Do you see a problem with inrush current at the controller?  I don't want to let the smoke out of a perfectly good one.... PS I currently use a 36vdc @60 farad super cap paralleled with my batteries and it helps the  battery drain tremendously with no inverter issues.  Thanks!

author: @ThisRandomUsername
text: The ideal thing to do if you want to minimise batteries and cost is have a house with a roof facing South East and another that faces South West in the Northern hemisphere. That flattens out the graph much the same as a tracking system at much less cost, but with a lot more area taken up. Obviously South facing is ideal for raw numbers, and could work if a lot of your power went into heating or cooling, as large low pressure water tanks are pretty cheap to build as well.

author: @Free-dv2vm
text: There's a gadget you can put on the greenhouse windo to open it when the temperature rises above a certain level. Just a mechanical arm with a timer. Could we not just use say a caravan timer to time these arms to adjust the position of the panel at certain time's of the day and the year to maximise the light harvested?

author: @G2startracker
text:
I've been interested in solar since ~1974, beginning with passive dwellings, then a crazy pilot told me his weird idea of reflecting sunlight throughout a house via the central air ducts and it got me thinking of guidance systems ~1976, by the 90's I had two patents for dual axis tracking, pro se.  In the 90's the numbers were -45%, depending on location, more power on an accurate tracker for PV's.  Thermal is the way to go, but if one must use PV's, the best way is to heat sink them to heat water because their optimum working range is -under- 100 degrees F., most get that hot within five minutes., yet hardly anyone does this, I've seen concentrated PV's, had to sign a nondisclosure, and even they were hardly heat sinking them to the air!  

A 1/3rd of the reason I moved to Vegas was the insolation charts, one of my trackers worked off the city water pressure, I got turned down at least three times for a grant from the DOE, (Bethesda, Colorado, & Sandia) the latter one said that it would need multiple redundancies because if it lost the articulation and guidance it would burn itself to the ground!  The senator got $12M to promote solar energy in Southern Nevada, I believe I was the only local at the numerous meetings that I would get my uninvite the day before, and dismissive reception, then they stated charging $375 admission.  They wouldn't even give me a test site, along with the Mayor Oscar Goodman, UNLV, The Desert Research, Nevada Power, and flood control, the only place offered was the Test Site depending on a $38,000 environmental impact study for one hole to protect the glowing Desert Tortoise.  I found out the hard way that if anything was happen, it depended on who had the best booze, hookers, drugs, and $ under the table, and they didn't care how long it would last as it would get tossed around like a red hot rivet and the gov't would pay off twice, because they are so expensive it can't fail.  

I still work on it, and now after collecting metal working machinery, it seems the DOM tubing is just as hard to locate as before, they wanted me to buy it by the railroad car!

author: @nunyabidniz2868

But that's just my 1st guess...

author: @nutblaster-g2r
text:
While commercial batteries might be expensive, there's good DIY solutions out there that can bring the cost to under 200€/kWh. I got my 14kWh LFP Battery for 2.5k including everything, I built the case myself but there's professional enclosures available for around 500€. 
I paid around 21k in total for my 10kWp ground mount system, and paid about 3.2k for the Panels and 2.5k for the battery. So the expensive stuff is actually the inverters/charge controllers and cables and not to forget all the little bit's and pieces. I paid more than 800€ for overvoltage protectors for example.

author: @NeoShameMan
text: What about tracking to reflector towards a fixed panel.

author: @SundogbuildersNet
text:
I seem to remember an article from many years ago. Perhaps it was in the Whole Earth Catalog (I'm dating myself now). Anyway, it was a passive system that used a refrigerant and pistons.  Supposedly, when set up properly, the system acted with solar heat on the piston barrels and the whole thing worked to keep its pistons constantly in the shade.
Maybe I'm confusing this with something else but I've often wondered what happened to that system. Must have been too expensive or something cause it pretty well disappeared and I've not seen any sign of it since.

text: Recently I read about a Solar farm that was going to flat mount the Solar panels. They said that with the lower cost of Solar panels, they could install more panels, flat mounted,  because they did not need to allow spacing between rows and they could save money on the land as well as the mounting/rack systems. They also decided they were going to use robots to keep the panels clean.

author: @AdricM
text: i recall a system where basically 2 tanks connected by some pipe  wwre mounted just haging off the east and westedge of  solar panel that can rock,  both tanks are painted black,  but the one in the sun will boil. and the one in the shade will condense and get heavy and tilt the panel bank until  the tank is out of the sun.   i guess the trickiest part is picking your vilitile liquid.  and dealing with seasonal changes..   and wind loading.

author: @mountiedm
text: Some carbon fiber and a couple of cheap electric motors. I do it just cause it's cool and I'm willing to pay for cool.

author: @ray_casso
text: One question how are you getting the air pressure going to move the bellows? Is it with an electric pump? Also I've built a 3,600kw folding Solar array on top of a size lift/cross chain anchored down to a 5 foot wide lazy susan on top of the roof of a 98 Ford expedition. And yes I have 250ft 8ga 4 wire SOO extension chord. As for tracking I've used 2 24v motors from a wheelchair. They got like 500 ftlbs of torque running to a 1 to 20 reduction gear, being powered by 4 12v 30w pannels.

author: @DanielSMatthews
text:
Visualise a solar PV array floating on a dam or lake, to keep cool and be more efficient, and to avoid wasting good land that is needed for vegetation. Have the array made of 5 pontoons, 4 corner ones that have a variable ballast system that allows for their height above the water to change, then simply by moving water around from one to the other you can orient the array which otherwise has most of its mass supported by the 5th central, and larger, pontoon. 

_(I place this invention into the public domain, if there is not already prior art.)_

replies.author: @peterrockell1527
replies.text: For a floating setup, a simpler method would be to rotate the raft to face the sun each day and then use bellows to adjust the altitude angle throughout the year, probably all controllable with a few LDR's and a micro controller.

replies.author: @DanielSMatthews
replies.text: @peterrockell1527 Not sure if that is simpler when that array is part of a huge grid of them covering the water. What mechanism is driving the rotation. Remember that the water transfer rate is very low, so you can use very simple methods, a sealed system and peristaltic pumping, unless you want to use the solar energy to drive a thermoacoustic pump feeding some fluid logic, thereby having only one moving part, water.

author: @geekswithfeet9137
text: The problem with solar tracking is that the frame costs just as much as a second panel. Doesn’t matter how cheap you make the tracking system.

author: @TgWags69
text: I always thought that a bimetalic spring array mounted in sealed mirror boxes around the edges of the panel could be used like the heliotropism effect only using the heat from the sun. As the array experiences changes in the angle of the sun, their will be a differential in which ones are exposed to sunlight/heat and the resulting temperature differential will return it to  balance. I'll leave it up to all the mechanical geniuses to figure out all the push me pull you bits to make it all work lol.

author: @AnalystPrime
text:
We can calculate literally years ahead when the Sun is going to rise and where it will be in relation to the solar panel for any location on Earth, yet somehow people keep saying solar is unpredictable.
And yes, I know weather remains an issue, but the people gleefully telling us that solar does not work at night as if they were the only ones to ever figure that out don't mean that it might get cloudy so you won't know exactly how many watts you get, they clearly imply the Sun might turn on and off like a badly connected lamp at any random time.
Only really unpredictable thing about it has been how everyone assumed solar panels will stay super expensive forever and spent lot of money on big tracking arrays, and then it became cheaper to just install a battery and more panels.

author: @rocketred55555
text: Like all ideas that sound great - but cloudy days? Heard of them? The temperature/energy available is variable so there is no possibility of consistent performance. This is high tech looking heliotropism is an answer looking for a problem.

author: @chrisgriffiths2533
text:
Australia has Installed Most of It's Solar Panels on Houses.
Most of the Houses are in the South and Recieve Less Sun than Central and Northern Australia.
We also have some Small Grid Scale Solar Fields and some of these are Stationary.
Whilst I am in Favour of House Based Solar and Highrise Solar.
It is a Fact that If the Exact Number of Solar Panels on Australian Houses was Installed in One of Australia's Northern Very Sunny Locations and They had Sun Tracking and Cooling Technology.  This Exact Number of Solar Panels would Produce at Least Double the Amount of Energy.
Good Topic.
Go Solar Go.

author: @fbuttstedt6577
text:

4 panels 1360W, 365 days tracking.
Beginning this month it reached 1700kwh for 2022.
Maintenance is zero until now, dc motors need to be replaced every 8 years, costs about €200

author: @smob0
text: I wonder if you could combine this with the giant floating solar duck technology from a few videos ago. Instead of one main hold for the water, you could split it into a few sections, and then have solonoid valves between them. You could use the voltage from the various panels to figure out where the sun is, and then fill the different water holds with different amounts of water to tip the duck towards the sun.

author: @toyotaboyhatman
text: even though tracking only adds 25%, the extra juice you get in the morning and dusk means less batteries which is the expensive part (not the panels).

replies.author: @vitordelima
replies.text: I'm almost sure concentrating solar power has a bigger improvement from using trackers.

author: @azinfidel6461
text:
Here in Arizona I have watched the development of trackers and debated for the past 20 years whether to add tracking to my solar setup. The expense and the risk of damage to my system never Justified it.
Fortunately,  with the advent of large used panels from failed commercial and  government subsidized solar projects panels are now very inexpensive. 
I recently picked up another 5Kw of panels from santan solar for only $850.
Problem solved.....

author: @paulbrouyere1735
text: You just reminded me of a solar waterheater I developed for kids. It has a solar parabole mirror reflecting sunlight on a tin can. Just some black paint, a couple of other cans and a glass jar or a PET bottle and you have yourself a solar boiler. But you reminded me I also developed a single axis solar follower, without electronics, with off the shelf cheap and easy to find materials. I guess I’ll better continue where I left it lying around. There is some logic involved but it really isn’t that complicated. Time to blow the dust of it…

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Blader idea is ingenious
I wonder if it could be made cheaply enough to hold up under wind loads on a roof. 
 
Dual axis tracking require extra space to minimize one pannel from early morning  and late evening shading another which further reduces the usefulness of dual axis tracking. One could still  2 axis track  in a densely packed array, but rhat will reduce late evening and early morning output, again reducing the usefulness of 2 axis tracking. Add significantly more complexity and cost and maintenance and 2 axis tracking looks pretty bad.

author: @gavinmorris154
text: Instead of setting up your axis parallel and perpendicular to the ground/house set 1 axis perpendicular to the suns apparent orbit, then you just need to rotate the panel from east through midday to west. This does not adjust for the 23degree seasonal variation. To follow the sun on a single axis and adjust for seasons youd also need to vary the angle of that single axis
